directors.name: Maya Hernandez
directors.bio: Maya Hernandez is an award-winning documentary filmmaker whose work focuses on climate justice, frontline communities, and intergenerational activism. Over the past fifteen years, she has directed and produced films that have screened at Sundance, Hot Docs, and IDFA, and have been used in classrooms and community organizing campaigns around the world. Her latest feature, "Our Climate Future," follows young organizers, scientists, and local leaders as they fight to transform their cities in the face of escalating climate impacts.

directors.name: Devon Choi
directors.bio: Devon Choi is a documentary producer with a background in investigative journalism and impact campaigns. They specialize in complex, cross-border stories about environmental policy, migration, and labor, and have led partnerships with NGOs, schools, and grassroots organizers to bring films into community spaces.

directors.name: Amina Diallo
directors.bio: Amina Diallo is a writer and story editor whose work bridges science communication and character-driven narrative. Trained in environmental policy and creative nonfiction, she collaborates closely with directors to shape emotionally resonant stories grounded in rigorous reporting.

directors.name: Lucas Ribeiro
directors.bio: Lucas Ribeiro is a cinematographer and co-creator on several of Maya Hernandez’s projects, known for intimate, observational camerawork that centers the daily lives of people on the frontlines of climate change.

films.title: Our Climate Future
films.synopsis: The feature documentary at the heart of this campaign, "Our Climate Future" follows three youth organizers, a coastal scientist, and a community elder as they work across generations to remake their cities in the face of rising seas, extreme heat, and political gridlock. Filmed over four years on three continents, the film blends intimate verité scenes with immersive climate storytelling to show what a just transition could look like.

films.title: Seeds of Tomorrow
films.synopsis: Set in urban farms and school gardens from Los Angeles to Detroit, "Seeds of Tomorrow" follows educators and teenagers reimagining food systems in neighborhoods long excluded from green space and fresh produce.

films.title: Beyond the Fence
films.synopsis: Filmed along the U.S.–Mexico border, this documentary traces how climate instability, trade policy, and militarized borders collide in the lives of families displaced by drought and crop failure.

films.title: City of Rising Tides
films.synopsis: In a coastal city racing the clock, tenants, city planners, and climate scientists wrestle with how to protect working-class neighborhoods as tidal flooding becomes a monthly reality.

films.title: After the Storm
films.synopsis: Shot in the year following a record-breaking hurricane, "After the Storm" focuses on a high school marching band, a neighborhood clinic, and a mutual aid kitchen as they navigate rebuilding and the politics of disaster relief.

films.title: Fault Lines
films.synopsis: An earlier feature by Maya Hernandez, "Fault Lines" explores how earthquake preparedness, housing policy, and economic inequality intersect in one West Coast metropolis sitting atop a major fault.

films.title: Listening to Fire
films.synopsis: Through the stories of Indigenous fire practitioners and rural firefighters, this film rethinks wildfires not only as disasters but as part of a living landscape shaped by culture, policy, and the climate crisis.

films.title: Groundwater
films.synopsis: "Groundwater" follows small farmers and community organizers in California's Central Valley as they contend with sinking land, contaminated wells, and the politics of who controls water in a drying state.

films.title: Sky Without Stars
films.synopsis: A more experimental short-form feature, "Sky Without Stars" weaves together night-sky timelapses, interviews with astronomers, and the stories of communities fighting light pollution and fossil fuel expansion near observatories.

reviews.title: ‘Our Climate Future’ Bridges Science and Grassroots Hope
reviews.author: Dr. Lena Ortiz
reviews.summary: A rare climate documentary that is as rigorous about the science as it is intimate with the people living its consequences.
reviews.content: By interweaving clear, accessible explanations of climate science with the daily lives of its protagonists, "Our Climate Future" avoids both fatalism and false optimism. The film becomes a moving primer on how climate policy, public health, and youth activism intersect in real neighborhoods.

reviews.title: A Ground-Level View of the Climate Fight
reviews.author: Caroline Park
reviews.summary: This urgent, empathetic documentary insists that the climate crisis is already here—and that ordinary people are rewriting its script.
reviews.content: Rather than centering experts in lab coats or politicians at podiums, "Our Climate Future" stays close to the youth organizers, nurses, and transit workers who feel climate disruption first. The result is a film that is less about catastrophe and more about the unruly, imperfect work of democracy.

reviews.title: Our Climate Future Review – Everyday Lives in an Age of Crisis
reviews.author: Amira Patel
reviews.summary: An unflashy but deeply affecting portrait of communities refusing to give up on the places they call home.
reviews.content: Maya Hernandez’s camera is patient and observant, lingering on small gestures—a shared meal during a blackout, a community meeting in a stuffy gym—that quietly reveal how people find one another in the dark. The film avoids simple heroes, and is stronger for it.

reviews.title: How ‘Our Climate Future’ Turns Policy Jargon into Human Stakes
reviews.author: Miguel Santos
reviews.summary: The film translates spreadsheets and zoning maps into the fate of families, block by block.
reviews.content: The genius of "Our Climate Future" lies in how it translates the opaque world of climate policy into the everyday questions people ask: Can I afford my asthma meds? Will this bus line still exist next year? Hernandez connects these dots without ever slipping into didacticism.

reviews.title: Our Climate Future Brings Urgency Without Despair
reviews.author: Chris Nolan
reviews.summary: A propulsive, character-driven climate doc that feels startlingly present-tense.
reviews.content: The film leans on a propulsive edit and an evocative score to keep the stakes high, but its most memorable moments are quiet: a teenager catching her breath before a speech, a nurse texting patients about a heat wave.

reviews.title: Ambitious Climate Doc Sometimes Sprawls, But Still Hits Hard
reviews.author: Joel Kim
reviews.summary: Not every storyline fully lands, but the film’s best sequences are bracingly alive.
reviews.content: With so many threads to juggle, some characters fade into the background, yet key set pieces—like a flooded subway commute and a tense city council vote—linger in the mind.

reviews.title: In ‘Seeds of Tomorrow,’ Teens Grow Food and Power
reviews.author: Naomi Reyes
reviews.summary: A vibrant look at food justice that also doubles as a love letter to public school teachers.
reviews.content: By centering school gardens and youth-led co-ops, the film reframes climate action as a daily practice rather than a distant policy fight.

videos.title: City Council Showdown on Climate Justice
videos.description: A tense 4-minute sequence where youth organizers confront city officials over a proposed gas plant.

videos.description: A short verité scene following students as they prepare handmade signs before a historic climate march.

videos.title: Night of the Flooded Subway
videos.description: A frontline nurse and transit worker navigate a flash flood that shuts down the city’s subway system.

videos.title: Building the First School Garden Bed
videos.description: Students and teachers construct the first raised bed in a parking-lot-turned-garden.
